coredata.dc:title: Preparation and multipurpose applications of nitrogen-sulfur doped carbon dots fluorescent probe for Ga3+ and riboflavin (VB2) detection
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Chan
Liang, Le
Liu, Yong-qing
Han, Mei
Wang, Xue-lin
Cai, Xiao-hua
Zou, Guang-long
coredata.dc:description: Designing versatile carbon quantum dots (CDs) with excellent sensitivity and unique function as efficient fluorescent probe for detection of specific substance from cheap and readily available substrates is still a crucial challenge. A novel nitrogen-sulfur doped carbon dots (N, S-CDs) with blue-green fluorescent emission derived from lactose and 4-amino-3-hydroxy-1-naphthalenesulfonic acid (AHNS) was facilely synthesized by one-step hydrothermal process. The obtained N, S-CDs with graphene structure exhibited good water solubility, excellent biocompatibility, satisfactory stability and striking optical properties. The surface of N, S-CDs is modified by various functional groups which are helpful to combine with Ga3+ in the form of complexes N, S-CDs-Ga3+ to considerably enhance the fluorescence emission peak of N, S-CDs, further improving the selectivity and sensitivity of Ga3+ detection with the detection limit of 0.133 μM. At the meantime, N, S-CDs can cause yellow fluorescence quenching of riboflavin (VB2) with the detection limit of 0.121 μM under the concentration range from 1.000-20.000 μM. The possible mechanism is the functional groups at the surface of N, S-CDs combined with the hydroxyl, amino and carbonyl group of VB2 by protonation and hydrogen bonds. Further investigations for practical applications demonstrated that N, S-CDs has good prospects in Ga3+ananlysis and detection of VB2 in food. Moreover, N, S-CDs can be used to determine whether there is Ga3+ in transferrin, which can indirectly inhibit the growth of cancer cells through the influence on transporting iron ion. Therefore, it is profitable for preliminary diagnosis and treatment of cancer cells in the field of biomedicine.

head.ce:title: Preparation and multipurpose applications of nitrogen-sulfur doped carbon dots fluorescent probe for Ga3+ and riboflavin (VB2) detection

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Guizhou Minzu University, Guiyang 550025, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, Guizhou Minzu University, Guiyang 550025, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Designing versatile carbon quantum dots (CDs) with excellent sensitivity and unique function as efficient fluorescent probe for detection of specific substance from cheap and readily available substrates is still a crucial challenge. A novel nitrogen-sulfur doped carbon dots (N, S-CDs) with blue-green fluorescent emission derived from lactose and 4-amino-3-hydroxy-1-naphthalenesulfonic acid (AHNS) was facilely synthesized by one-step hydrothermal process. The obtained N, S-CDs with graphene structure exhibited good water solubility, excellent biocompatibility, satisfactory stability and striking optical properties. The surface of N, S-CDs is modified by various functional groups which are helpful to combine with Ga3+ in the form of complexes N, S-CDs-Ga3+ to considerably enhance the fluorescence emission peak of N, S-CDs, further improving the selectivity and sensitivity of Ga3+ detection with the detection limit of 0.133 μM. At the meantime, N, S-CDs can cause yellow fluorescence quenching of riboflavin (VB2) with the detection limit of 0.121 μM under the concentration range from 1.000-20.000 μM. The possible mechanism is the functional groups at the surface of N, S-CDs combined with the hydroxyl, amino and carbonyl group of VB2 by protonation and hydrogen bonds. Further investigations for practical applications demonstrated that N, S-CDs has good prospects in Ga3+ananlysis and detection of VB2 in food. Moreover, N, S-CDs can be used to determine whether there is Ga3+ in transferrin, which can indirectly inhibit the growth of cancer cells through the influence on transporting iron ion. Therefore, it is profitable for preliminary diagnosis and treatment of cancer cells in the field of biomedicine.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Gallium is a scarce metal mainly derives from zinc ore, bauxite, coal ash, boehmite, sphalerite and soil [1–4]. Gallium and its derivatives are widely used in electronic devices such as solar cells and semiconductor light-emitting diodes (LEDs) due to their unique photoelectric properties [5–8]. Ga3+ also exhibits satisfactory appetency in tumor-targeting, such as gallium nitrate can act as an antitumor drug in clinical diagnosis and practice [9–11]. With the emerging development in high-tech industries in recent years, it is inevitably for the release of gallium into the surroundings. Gallium contamination may expose health risks to humans through the food chain because its accumulation or exceeded the safety range in the human body which can interfere with iron metabolism and cause a broad variety of serious health problems including testicular toxicity, anemia, osteoclastic bone resorption, protein synthesis inhibition, etc. [12–16]. Therefore, it is of great significance to set up a method for Ga3+ detection in different circumstances and media. The methods for Ga3+ detection include atomic absorption spectroscopy[17], voltammetry[18], atomic emission spectrum[19], spectrophotometry [20], electrical impedance spectroscopy [21], neutron activation analysis [22], etc. But these methods have many disadvantages such as complicated operation, high cost and tedious procedures [23].

Riboflavin (VB2) as a water-soluble vitamin plays a crucial role in human health, which can involve in the metabolism of protein, carbohydrates, ketone bodies and fat to stimulate cell growth and development [24,25]. Insufficient intake of VB2 can bring metabolic disorder diseases including keratitis, conjunctivitis, tongue inflammation, and seborrheic dermatitis [26,27]. VB2 needs to be supplied through food or nutrition because it is difficult to be prepared in the human body. VB2 has widely been used as a nutritional supplement for animals and humans in the past decades [28]. Up till now, the detection of VB2 includes electrochemical method, microbial detection, enzyme-linked immunoassay and so on. Most of these methods usually are time-consuming and complicated in experimental operation. It is essential to establish simple and effective VB2 detection systems in food and life science [29–31].

Fluorescent sensors have become powerful tools for detection of ions and molecules in recent decades owing to its rapid response, inexpensive, simplicity, high selectivity and sensitivity, and versatility [32,33]. As a new type of fluorescent nanomaterials with a particle size of less than 10 nm, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted great interest in view of its high photostability, satisfactory water solubility, low toxicity, excellent biocompatibility and splendid optical properties [34–39]. Although fluorescent sensors from CDs have rapidly made satisfactory achievements in the field of ions detection, molecules detection and other applicants over recent years [40–51]. However, for all we know, there are only fewer examples for detection of Ga3+ and VB2 based on CDs. In 2015, Zou and coworkers reported a nitrogen-doped graphitic CDs using Ga(NO3)3 as carbon and nitrogen sources for detection of Ga3+ with a detection limit of 0.209 μM [52]. Xiao et al also developed a nitrogen-doped CDs for Ga3+ detection employing ammonium citrate as carbon and nitrogen sources [53]. Lin et al developed a nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped CDs to VB2 with a detection limit of 0.17 μmol/L in the concentration range from 0.5–50 μmol/L [29]. In 2022, Cong group prepared a mussels-derived CDs for bio-imaging and detection of VB2 [54]. Medina and coworkers described a radiometric fluorescent sensor for sequential on–off-on VB2 detection [55]. Song group achieved a B and N Co-doped carbon dots with radiometric fluorescence detection of VB2 and evaluated its application in information security [56]. Monte-Filho synthesized fluorescent CDs for determination of VB2 in multivitamin/mineral supplements using lemon and onion juices as the substrates [57]. Therefore, it is noteworthy to develop novel, simple and efficient CDs for Ga3+ and VB2 detection system.

CDs based on biological sources have attracted wide concerns due to their low-cost, high safety and excellent biocompatibility in recent years. Lactose is one of the disaccharides, whose molecular structure is formed by the condensation of glucose and galactose. 4-amino-3-hydroxy-1-naphthalenesulfonic acid (AHNS) is a derivative of aniline containing three functional groups (–NH2, –OH and −SO3H) to provide binding sites between metallic ions and organic small molecules. The amino group easily reacts with the free aldehyde group of lactose to form the imine (−C=N) structure. In this work, a facile and novel nitrogen-sulfur doped carbon dots (N, S-CDs) with light blue-green fluorescent emission derived from lactose and AHNS was obtained by one-step hydrothermal method. The N, S-CDs exhibits unique optical performance and can efficiently identify and detect Ga3+ and VB2 with higher selectivity and sensitivity. In addition, the plausible combination mechanism between N, S-CDs and Ga3+ or VB2 and its potential applications are also investigated.

Lactose and AHNS were obtained from Shanghai McLean Biochem. Technol. Co., Ltd. VB2 was supplied by Jakes Chem. Co. Ltd. All the chemical reagents were used in the experiment without further purification. The microstructures of the CDs were characterized by transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL2010). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on a thermos Scientific K-α spectrometer with an Al Kα excitation source to evaluate the surface chemical state of the material. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were examined on a thermos Scientific Nicolet iS50 spectrometer with range from 4000 − 550 cm−1, and 64 scans were performed in the transmission mode for each spectrum. Fluorescence spectra were collected using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-320 Tianjin Guangdong Technology Co., LTD., China). The ultraviolet absorption spectra of samples were acquired from the TU1810 spectrometer (Beijing Per See Instrument Co., LTD., China).

Lactose (0.5 g) and AHNS (0.5 g) were dissolved in water (10 mL) under ultrasound, then 5 ml of 2 % hydrochloric acid was added dropwise to form homogenous solution. The aforementioned solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180°C for 10 h. After the completion of reaction, the solution was filtered using 0.220 μm filtration membrane, the obtained N, S-CDs solution was stored in a refrigerator at 4°C for further use, and the N, S-CDs powder was collected through drying supernatant in the oven at 50 °C for structural characterization.

The fluorescence quantum yield of N, S-CDs is followed by comparison method [58] employing quinine sulfate as the standard reference. The calculated quantum yield of N, S-CDs is 16.7 % by formula (1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: N, S-CDs for detection of Ga3+ and VB2
ce:section.ce:para: The different concentrations solution of Ga(NO3)3 was added into the diluted N,S-CDs solution for the fluorescence measurement at the maximum excitation (λex = 350 nm) and emission wavelength (λem = 495 nm). N, S-CDs solution with variable concentrations was added into VB2 solution for fluorescence quenching measurement. The synthetic process for N, S-CDs and illustration for detection of Ga3+ and VB2 were presented in Scheme 1 .

The morphology of the prepared N, S-CDs was characterized through transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM). It can be observed from Fig. 1
a that N, S-CDs are basically uniform and well dispersed. The HRTEM image of N, S-CDs clearly shows the spacing of lattice fringes (0.208 nm), which is very similar to the diffraction plane of graphene, indicating that N, S-CDs have a graphene-like structure. It can be seen from Fig. 1 b that the average particle size of N, S-CDs is about 2.090 nm, and the distribution range is narrow. The composition and structure of N, S-CDs were surveyed by infrared spectroscopy (Fig. 1 c). For pure lactose, the apparent peak at 3378 cm−1 was assigned to the stretching vibration of O-H, the absorption band at 2898 cm−1 was ascribed to CH bond stretching vibration, and the peak at 1261 cm−1 was attributed to CO bond vibration. For AHNS, the distinct peaks at 3442 cm−1 and 3376 cm−1 are derived from the vibrations OH and NH bonds, while the double peaks at 1138 cm−1 are ascribed to SO asymmetric stretching vibrations, which is split into double peaks. For N, S-CDs, a broad peak near 3155 cm−1 is due to the association hydrogen bonds between OH and NH, indicating the presence of amino and hydroxyl functional groups. The sharp peaks at 1713 cm−1 and 1399 cm−1 are the vibration of the CO and C-N bond respectively, suggesting that lactose is bound to the amino group in AHNS. The reduction of SO peaks at 1167 cm−1 is credited with environmental changes, indicating a reaction between lactose chain and AHNS. The peak at 1059 cm−1 is caused by COC vibration, and the wide vibration at 592 cm−1 indicates the presence of CH2, which indicates the formation of N, S-CDs [59,60]. The results of FT-IR analysis confirm that N, S-CDs surface containing unsaturated carbon structure and oxygen, nitrogen, sulfur groups, and also indicate N, S-CDs are successfully prepared. These functional groups not only improve the stability and hydrophilicity of N, S-CDs in aqueous solution, but also interact with metal ions or molecules. Although there are no distinct C-S or S-O bands in the FT-IR spectra of N, S-CDs, XPS spectra were further used to confirm the binding of sulfur to the structure of N, S-CDs.

In order to further verified the results obtained by FT-IR, XPS spectra were conducted to examine the functional groups and chemical compositions of synthesized N, S-CDs. As illustrated in Fig. 1 d. The representative peaks at 284.91 eV, 402.09 eV, 532.52 eV, and 169.34 eV are corresponding to C1s, N1s, O1s and S2p, respectively. The element proportion of N, S-CDs is carbon 74.49 %, nitrogen 2.14 %, oxygen 21.04 %, and sulfur 2.32 %. The high-resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. 1 e) shows three peaks located at 284.94 eV, 286.25 eV and 288.84 eV, corresponding to the C–C/C–S/C=C, C–N, and C=O/C–O–C/C–O bonds, respectively. The C–C sp2 characteristic peak at 284.94 eV indicates that the synthesized N, S-CDs should contain abundant graphite structure. The peaks at 400.38 eV and 402.14 eV of N1s can be attributed to the C-N-C and N-H groups, respectively (Fig. 1 f). The peaks at 532.19 eV and 533.29 eV of O1s (Fig. 1 g) are indicative of C=O and C-O-C/C-OH groups [61]. The high-resolution spectrum of S2p exhibits three peaks at 168.59 eV, 169.20 eV and 170.46 eV, corresponding to S-O/S=O, S2p3/2, and S2p1/2, respectively (Fig. 1 h). Both XPS and FT-IR data confirm that S and N atoms have effectively been doped into CDs, and N, S-CDs is decorated with the functional groups such as oxygen, nitrogen and sulfur.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical performances of N, S-CDs and detection of Ga3+
ce:section.ce:para:
The optical characteristics of N, S-CDs were surveyed by UV and fluorescence spectra tests. The UV–vis absorption of N, S-CDs is depicted in Fig. 2
a, a distinct absorption peak is observed at 330 nm, which is ascribed to the π–π* transition of C=O [55,62]. In the meantime, the maximum excitation and emission wavelength of N, S-CDs is 465 nm and 495 nm, respectively. The aqueous solution of N, S-CDs glows a faint blue fluorescence under ultraviolet light at 365 nm, and it is transparent pale yellow under visible light. The corresponding spectra for fluorescence emission of N, S-CDs at different excitation wavelengths were presented in Fig. 2 b. It is obvious that the emission of N, S-CDs is coherently related to the excitation wavelength. The maximum emission wavelength moves from 475 nm to 495 nm when the excitation wavelength gradually increases from 350 nm to 440 nm. This may arise from different particle sizes of N, S-CDs and multiple surface emission sites. It should be pointed out that the fluorescence intensity of N, S-CDs initially increases and then decreases followed red-shift wavelength with the increase of excitation wavelength, the fluorescence intensity appears the maximum value when the excitation wavelength is 400 nm. The tunability of N, S-CDs fluorescence may be ascribed to the heterogeneity of particle size and a great deal of surface state emission traps formed by hydroxyl, carboxyl, amino and sulfonic functional groups [63,64].

Owing to the abundant functional groups on the surface of N, S-CDs and excellent optical properties, the application of N, S-CDs are also further explored. In order to evaluate the selectivity for N, S-CDs as fluorescent probe to Ga3+ detection, a serial of different metal ions with equivalent concentration was added into N, S-CDs solution, and the effects of metal ions on the fluorescence intensity of N, S-CDs were recorded at room temperature. The experimental results (Fig. 2 c) showed that the addition of Mg2+, Al3+, Ca2+, Na+, Zn2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Co2+, Cd2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Cr3+, Mn2+ had barely influence on the fluorescence intensity and the solution of N, S-CDs exhibits weak fluorescence. In order to assess the selectivity of the N, S-CDs system for Ga3+ detection, different cations were added to the N, S-CDs-Ga3+ system as shown in Fig. S1 . When Ga3+ is added, the fluorescence of the N, S-CDs-Ga3+ system solution rapidly changes from light blue to blue-green within one minute ( Fig. S2 ).

(b) Excitation-dependent fluorescence spectra of N, S-CDs; (c) Fluorescence spectra of various metal ions (400 μM) in the solution of N, S-CDs; (d) Fluorescence spectra and error analysis of various metal ions (400 μM) in N, S-CDs solution; (e) Fluorescence emission diagram of N, S-CDs with the increasing of Ga3+ concentration (0–400 μM); (f) The nonlinear Stern-Volmer increasing curve of N, S-CDs for Ga3+ concentrations between 0 and 400 μM, the inset shows the linear part of the Stern-Volmer enhancing curve for Ga3+ ion concentrations between 20 and 80 μM.

The fluorescence intensity of N, S-CDs-Ga3+ system was observed at 273 k, 288 k and 298 k. When the pH value is between 1.0 and 9.0, the fluorescence of N, S-CDs-Ga3+ system has almost no change, but there is no recognition effect to Ga3+ when the pH value of N, S-CDs exceeds 9.0 ( Fig. S3 ). Therefore, suitable pH range for N, S-CDs to detection of Ga3+ is pH=1.0–9.0. Contrary to other metal ions, Ga3+ ions significantly enhanced the fluorescence intensity of the solution of N, S-CDs as shown in Fig. 2 d. Various ions with same concentrations including Mg2+, Al3+, Ca2+, Na+, Zn2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Co2+, Cd2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Cr3+, Mn2+ are added to 50 μM of N, S-CDs solution. The results show that co-existence of other ions only has negligible effect on the fluorescence intensity, and indicates that N, S-CDs can selectively detect Ga3+ in the mixture and the other metal ions have no interference. In addition, the UV–vis spectra of N, S-CDs in the presence of various metal ions were studied. As shown in Fig. S4 , after adding 50 μM of Ga3+, the UV–vis absorption of N, S-CDs significantly changes, and the absorption disappears at 327 nm. To the contrary, when other metal ions with identical concentration is added, the absorption spectrum is almost unchanged. The possible reason is that the surface groups of N, S-CDs chelate with Ga3+, which affects the surface state of N, S-CDs. These results potently prove that N, S-CDs as fluorescent probes have preferable selectivity for Ga3+.

In order to verify the sensitivity of N, S-CDs for Ga3+ detection, the standard curve of Ga3+ was established under the optimal conditions. As shown in Fig. 2 e, with the increase of Ga3+ concentration, the fluorescence intensity of N, S-CDs also increases, and the enhancement value of fluorescence intensity (Y) is linearly correlated with the concentration of Ga3+ (X). When the Ga3+ concentration is between 20–80 μM, the linear regression equation is Y=3.5572X+166.8872, R 2 = 0.99712. According to the acquainted equation “LOD=3σ/k” (S/N=3)[65], the limit of detection can be calculated to be 0.133 μM (Fig. 2 f), dramatically lower than recently reported detection methods for N, S-CDs based on different precursors[66]. Therefore, N, S-CDs can be used as a fluorescent probe to detect Ga3+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism for N, S-CDs to detection of Ga3+
ce:section.ce:para:
Although the exact fluorescence enhancement mechanism is still unclear, it could be attributed to the metal ions combination with amino, hydroxyl, sulfonic functional groups on the surface N, S-CDs, which facilitate the formation of complexes with Ga3+ and change the optical properties of N, S-CDs. As can be watched from Fig. 3
a, the peak of the CO bond at 1713 cm−1 becomes duller after the addition of Ga3+, and SO bond at 1167 cm−1 changes from a wide peak to a narrow peak and moves to high frequency, which can be ascribed to the fact that the addition of Ga3+ changes the environment of N, S-CDs. At 872 cm−1, the peak of SO single bond becomes weaker, which may be due to the combination of Ga3+ and oxygen de-electron on SO single bond. The anticipated peak at 645 cm−1 and 563 cm−1 is the stretching vibration of NGa3+ and OGa3+ bond, respectively, which directly leads to the change of connection mode among the functional groups in N, S-CDs and promotes the fluorescence emission. In order to further confirm the above results, the functional groups and chemical components of N, S-CDs-Ga3+ system were analyzed by XPS. As shown in Fig. 3 b, XPS spectra reflect the appearance of gallium elements on the surface of CDs when Ga3+ was incorporated, there is a new emerging peak at 1118.96 eV corresponding to Ga3+ compared with the original N, S-CDs. According to Fig. 3 c, upon the addition of Ga3+, there is a new peak at 161.17 eV in the high-resolution S2p spectrum, which may be assigned to S-O-Ga. The high-resolution Ga 2p spectrum could be fitted into two secondary peaks at 1145 eV and 1118 eV corresponding to two 2p orbits ( Fig. S5 ), indicating successful incorporation of N, S-CDs by gallium.

According to Fig. 3 d, there are diverse functional groups including hydroxyl, amino and sulfonic acid on the surface of N, S-CDs, which can easily bind to Ga3+ to form complexes. To meet the requirement of six coordination numbers of Ga3+ ion, two of N, S-CDs can combine with one Ga3+ to form stable complex which stimulate blue-green fluorescence enhancement. When Ga3+ is added, N, S-CDs and Ga3+ form a complex to cause the detachment of Ga3+ d-orbital to make the excited electronic state of N, S-CDs to transfer to the d -orbital of Ga3+. The electronic transition of N, S-CDs from the recognition group to the fluorescent group affected by Ga3+ at combination of sites to results in fluorescence opening via photo-induced electron transfer. In order to confirm this speculation, based on the modified Senesi-Hildebrand equation, the complexation constants of N, S-CDs with Ga3+ and common metal ions at the same temperature are calculated:
(2)

F0 is the fluorescence intensity of N, S-CDs.
Δ F
is the fluorescence change of N, S-CDs after the addition of Ga3+. K is the binding constant and n is the complexation ratio.

The binding constant of Ga3+ calculated by formula (2) and (3) is 3.6 × 105 M−1. Compared with other ions, it is significantly larger ( Table S1
), indicating that the binding between N, S-CDs and Ga3+ is very stable and can be used to specifically recognize Ga3+ [67,68].

Therefore, the high selectivity for N, S-CDs to Ga3+ detection may be regarded as the mutual interaction of various functional groups on the surface of N, S-CDs with Ga3+ that beneficial to energy transfer. Meanwhile, N atom has a large electron negativity, which is conducive to enhancing the electron density distribution and facilitating the complexation of Ga3+ with surface functional groups of N, S-CDs [69,70].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism for N, S-CDs to VB2 identification
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to investigate the effect of N, S-CDs on the fluorescence intensity of VB2, a serial of various concentrations of N, S-CDs solution were added to 1.0 mg/L of VB2 solution. As shown in Fig. 4
a, when 1 ml of N, S-CDs solution is added to VB2 solution, the fluorescence of VB2 solution is gradually quenched with the concentration rising, and completely disappears eventually. As mentioned earlier, the addition of Ga3+ can enhance the fluorescence intensity of N, S-CDs solution, which may quench the fluorescence of VB2 solution. To optimize the amount of N, S-CDs to determination of VB2, a 50 mg/L concentration of VB2 solution (2 ml) was added to 2 ml of different concentrations of N, S-CDs solution to investigate the changes in fluorescence intensities. As shown in Fig. S6 , the results indicated that the fluorescence of VB2 was completely quenched when the concentration of N, S-CDs was 50 mg/L. Interestingly, the quenching solution of N, S-CDs and VB2 can efficiently resume when the addition of Ga3+ solution. The fluorescence intensities of N, S-CDs, N, S-CDs-Ga3+, and VB2, N, S-CDs-VB2 systems formed a sharp contrast ( Fig.S7 ). The strong fluorescence of VB2 was rapidly quenched within one minute ( Fig.S8 ). Based on these findings, N, S-CDs as a universal sensitive sensor system can be applied for the determination of Ga3+ and trace VB2.

Under optimal conditions, the sensitivity of N, S-CDs to VB2 tracking was measured. The fluorescence intensity of VB2 at 530 nm decreased with the increase of N, S-CDs concentration, and exhibits a nonlinear Stern-Volmer quenching curve of VB2 when N, S-CDs concentration is between 0 and 70 mg/L. The fluorescence quenching cannot be linearly fitted to the entire N, S-CDs concentration range, but there is a good linear relationship in the concentration range from 10 to 55 mg/L as shown in Fig. 4 b. The linear regression equation is Y=–22.41251X+1170.68 (R 2 = 0.99107), the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 0.121 μM. The methods demonstrated many advantages including lower detection limit and visual effect, and can be used to track the ingredients in complicate food samples. As can be seen from Fig. 4 c, N, S-CDs-VB2 forms two absorption peaks at 3480 cm−1 and 3096 cm−1 other than one wide absorption peak of original N, S-CDs at 3155 cm−1, the absorption at 3480 cm−1 is assigned to hydroxyl groups in VB2 molecular. The absorption peak of the carboxyl group bond at 1713 cm−1 almost disappeared, which may be regarded as VB2 combination with the carboxyl group. In addition, it is not difficult to observe that the absorption peak at 1166 cm−1 becomes two narrow absorption peaks, and the missing SO bond is reassembled, and a new peak appears at 951 cm−1, which may be because the addition of VB2 increases the number of CN bonds, resulting in an obvious peak. The combination of VB2 with N, S-CDs is confirmed by IR spectroscopy, which causes the fluorescence quenching of VB2. In order to investigate the fluorescence stability of N, S-CDs-VB2 system, the relationship between the fluorescence ratio of N, S-CDs (F0) and N, S-CDs-VB2 (Fi) and pH value was studied. It can be seen that when pH reaches 10, the ratio is very small ( Fig. S9 ), indicating that N, S-CDs to detection of VB2 is suitable at acidic, neutral and weak basic environments.

To confirm the presence of electron transfer, the energy levels of HOMO and LUMO molecular orbital of N, S-CDs are calculated using the following empirical formula:
(4)

+ 4.4
(5)

E
ox
+ 4.4

Eox and Ered represent the oxidation and reduction potentials of N, S-CDs, respectively. Combined with the ultraviolet spectrum analysis direct band gap (Eg ) of N, S-CDs, the Eg is calculated to be 3.92 eV as shown in Fig. S10 . The Ered is measured as − 0.341 eV by cyclic voltammetry as shown in Fig. S11 . In view of the irreversibility of the oxidation process, Eox cannot be directly obtained from Eq. (5). Then, the Eox of N, S-CDs can be obtained by formula (6):
(6)

E
LUMO

E g

Thus, the EHOMO of N, S-CDs is estimated to be −7.98 eV. According to the literature[71], the EHOMO and ELUMO of VB2 are − 6.28 eV and − 2.82 eV, respectively, Since the LUMO orbital energy level of VB2 is higher than that of N, S-CDs (Fig. 4 d), blocking the passage of the excited electron of N,S-CDs − VB2 to the LUMO orbital of VB2. Therefore, photoinduced electron transfer (PET) cannot occur between N, S-CDs and VB2. The strong yellow fluorescence of VB2 can be quenched by the N, S-CDs solution because alkaline nitrogen atom (N=C-) at the ring of VB2 is easily protonated by acidic SO3H. Moreover, its lactam structure also may act with amino and hydroxyl groups from the surface of N, S-CDs by hydrogen bond and weakens the conjugated system of VB2 to cause fluorescence quenching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of N, S-CDs as Ga3+ and VB2 probes in diverse circumstances
ce:section.ce:para:
Although gallium is an indispensable raw material in semiconductor industry, it is necessary to develop a fast and feasible method to detect gallium in view of its potential risk to the environment and human health. As shown in Fig. 5
a, the stock solution transitions from light blue to bright blue-green fluorescence upon addition of Ga3+ ion into N, S-CDs solution under UV illumination. In the meantime, the bright yellow fluorescence of VB2 solution was quenched after the addition of N, S-CDs. In order to improve the practicability for N, S-CDs to detection of Ga3+, a solid phase hydrogel detection system from polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) was developed based on the fluorescence enhancement of N, S-CDs when the addition of Ga3+ [72]. The smaller particle size and prominent optical stability of N, S-CDs allow them to be evenly distributed in the hydrogel system under various conditions. On the other hand, the elastic three-dimensional lattice structure of the hydrogel provides sufficient space for the doping of N, S-CDs. As seen from Fig. 5 b, solid-state hydrogel sensor chips doped with N, S-CDs shows light blue fluorescence. After the addition of Ga3+, the fluorescent color sensibly changed from light blue to blue-green (Fig. 5 c). The other anti-interference cations were dropped into the aforementioned solution, and its fluorescence intensity and color were hardly different (Fig. 5 d). Furthermore, it can also be applied to test paper as an anti-counterfeiting mark ( Fig. S12 ). In a word, the solid phase hydrogel system derived from nitrogen-sulfur doped CDs is beneficial to the detection of Ga3+.

It is noted that Ga3+ can easily bind to transferrin (Tf), then based on Tf-Ga3+ and S, N-CDs system tests were conduct in vitro. Cancer cells are attached to iron transported by Tf in plasma, Tf will bind to Ga3+when the addition of Ga3+ in view of iron and Ga3+ possessing similar electronic cloud. When addition of Ga3+, it can promote the absorption of gallium not actually needed iron in proliferating cancer cells. Therefore, Ga3+ has an important effect on the survival of cancer cells and can lead to the disintegration and death of cancer cells. There is no fluorescence display in pure Tf crystal system (Fig. 5 e), but a transparent Tf-Ga3+ crystal is observed under both sunlight and UV light (Fig. 5 f). A yellow crystal spot was found in the sample center, which is due to the fact that the Tf powder is not completely dissolved in the Ga3+ solution and thus make metamorphism, but it does not hinder observation at the edge of Tf film. The two Tf crystal films show the same light blue fluorescence (Fig. 5 g) without and with addition of N, S-CDs solution. Contrary to our expectations, the color of Tf-Ga3+ crystal film gradually changed from light blue to blue-green (Fig. 5 h) after incorporation of N, S-CDs solution. The fluorescence of Tf crystal films in the presence and absence of Ga3+ solution was compared as shown in Fig. 5 h. It can observe a small amount of N, S-CDs solution seep into added to the edge of the slide within 30 s, the solution slowly invaded the Tf from the left to the right outer edge, indicating that the N, S-CDs solution had good penetrating ability to Tf, and its depth was comparable to that of water. Therefore, the interaction N, S-CDs with gallium is helpful to investigate anticancer properties based on Tf in organisms or humans [73]. From this, it is possible to detect whether there is Ga3+ in Tf in transporting ions, which can indirectly inhibit the growth of cancer cells and even make cancer cells apoptosis without energy demand. Therefore, N, S-CDs can be used to detect whether Tf binds to Ga3+ and is helpful for brief diagnosis and treatment of cancer cells based on Tf. pH changes to fluorescent protein probe are very important, however, most of reported fluorescent probe for Tf detection can only be used at weak acidic and neutral conditions (pH=4.8–7.4) to limit their biological experiments[74–76]. Suitable pH range for N, S-CDs to detection of Ga3+ in Tf is pH=1.0–9.0. Therefore, it may have letter application values in both medicine and organisms.

In order to verify the feasibility of the prepared N, S-CDs sensor for the detection of VB2 in actual samples, Vitasoy and Grain milk drinking beverages on the market were selected as actual samples for analysis. According to the ingredient list, the level of VB2 in both samples was 8 %. The test sample was diluted 10 times with deionized water for measurement. The detailed emission fluorescence spectra of Vitasoy and Grain milk drink were obtained at 525 nm excitation wavelength. The fluorescence intensity of Vitasoy and Grain milk drink were 38 and 35, respectively. The fluorescence peaks of Vitasovic and Grain milk drink at 523 nm disappeared after addition 4 μM N, S-CDs solutions ( Fig. S13 and S14 ). Therefore, it is feasible to detect VB2 in practical samples using N, S-CDs. In order to investigate the recovery rate of N, S-CDs from the two commercial beverages, a serial of different concentrations of N, S-CDs solution was added into the two samples, and the experimental results were tabulated in table S2 . The recoveries of Vitasoy and Grain milk drink were 98.25 %, 97.30 % and 98.65 %, 97.22 %, respectively, and their relative standard deviation (RSD) were no more 5 %. These studies demonstrate that the prepared N, S-CDs can be used to tracksVB2 in food samples.

In summary, a novel N, S-CDs was developed based on inexpensive substrates lactose and AHNS by a simple hydrothermal synthetic strategy. N, S-CDs with graphene-like structure and average size of 2.090 nm exhibits excellent water solubility, biocompatibility and satisfactory stability. The surface of N, S-CDs containing amino, hydroxyl and sulfonate functional groups can selectively and sensitively identify and detect Ga3+ and VB2 in aqueous solution, whose limit of detection are 0.133 μM and 0.121 μM, respectively. The possible mechanisms for identification of Ga3+ and VB2 are that the functional groups on the surface of N, S-CDs directly combined with Ga3+ or interacts with the C=N, C=O and N-H groups in VB2 molecule through protonation and hydrogen bonds. Investigations for practical applications confirmed that N, S-CDs may have good prospects in fluorescence analysis and detection of Ga3+ in environment and VB2 in food. Moreover, N, S-CDs can be used to detect whether there is Ga3+ in transferrin and is profitable for preliminary diagnosis and treatment of cancer cells in the field of biomedicine.
